chunk_summary: Fortified rice in India contains iron, folic acid, and vitamin B12 within regulated ranges, supports prevention of deficiencies, and requires proper cooking methods to retain nutrients.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutrient composition of fortified rice under Indian standards.
Learn cooking methods to maximize micronutrient retention in fortified rice.
Differentiate fortified rice from therapeutic iron treatments in terms of dosage and purpose.
Recognize labeling and safety nuances for fortified rice products.
key_facts: Fortified rice contains iron (28–42.5 mg/kg for ferric pyrophosphate or 14–21.25 mg/kg for sodium iron EDTA), folic acid (75–125 mcg/kg), and vitamin B12 (0.75–1.25 mcg/kg).
A 100 g serving of fortified rice provides 2.8–4.25 mg of iron from ferric pyrophosphate.
Therapeutic iron for a 10 kg child is approximately 30 mg/day, far higher than fortified rice iron content.
Micronutrient retention is better with absorption cooking (water not discarded) than boiling and decanting.
Fortified rice packaging includes the +F logo and “fortified with …” statement, but caution labels for hemoglobinopathies are no longer mandatory.
reference_quote: Under Indian fortification standards, fortified rice contains iron, folic acid, and vitamin B12 within specified ranges per kg. (Source: FSSAI Compendium of Food Fortification Regulations)
document_key_topics: Counseling on fortified rice benefits and limitations
Labeling and safety considerations for fortified rice
